text.title: A/B Testing Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every A/B runtime must obey. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Variant assignment MUST be a deterministic function of (user_id, experiment_id, salt) via 64-bit hash mod 100. random.choice / time-based assignment / per-request RNG are forbidden.

rationale.source: Microsoft ExP guidance + Internal — A/B audits 2024

rule.rationale: Non-deterministic assignment breaks sticky bucketing; the same user gets different variants on different requests, destroying signal.

rule.statement: The bucket function MUST yield the same variant for the same user_id across web, iOS, Android, email, and server-side callbacks. Surface-local randomness is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Cross-surface variant drift produces an inconsistent user experience and biases conversion attribution.

rule.statement: Every experiment-run MUST attach an SRM check (chi-square p > 0.001 against expected split). Failed SRM invalidates the result; ship/kill decisions are blocked.

rationale.source: Kohavi et al. — Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments

rule.rationale: SRM is the canonical signal that bucketing leaked or one variant filtered users out; ignoring it produces wrong conclusions that look statistically significant.

rule.statement: Conversion events MUST only count after an exposure event has fired for the same (user_id, experiment_id). Conversions without prior exposure are dropped.
rule.rationale: Counting conversions without exposure measures the population, not the experiment; gives the false impression that the variant works on users who never saw it.

rule.statement: Decision artefact MUST report Wilson 95% CI on the conversion-rate delta, not raw percentages. A point-estimate without CI is invalid.

rationale.source: Wilson 1927 + modern stats engine output norms

rule.rationale: Raw percentages hide variance; small samples can show "10% lift" with a CI spanning -5% to +25%, i.e. no decision possible.
